excerpt: "In the beginning" does not denote a beginning in time but signals that creation itself had a starting point — that before it, there was simply God.
full_text: Basil writes in the Hexaemeron: 'In the beginning God created.' The word 'beginning' is used here not in the sense of a temporal moment that preceded others, but as the first point from which the created order proceeded. Before this beginning there was no time, no silence, no rest — for all of these require time, and time had not yet been made. God, dwelling in eternity, brought forth both time and the world together in a single creative act.
source: Hexaemeron, Homily 1

excerpt: God made heaven and earth not in time but with time, for time itself is a creature and began with the world.
full_text: Augustine argues in the Confessions and City of God that the question 'What was God doing before He made heaven and earth?' is malformed, because before creation there was no 'before' — time itself came into being with the world. God is eternal, not subject to sequence, and His creative act was not an event within time but the origin of time itself. 'In the beginning God created' means the beginning of all things, including temporal succession.
source: Confessions, Book XI; City of God, Book XI

excerpt: Moses writes 'In the beginning' to show that the world is not eternal but has a maker who transcends it.
full_text: Ambrose in Hexaemeron notes that Moses begins with 'In the beginning' specifically to refute those philosophers who held the world to be eternal and uncreated. By asserting a beginning, Moses declares a Creator who stands outside and prior to the created order. The heavens and the earth owe their existence entirely to God's free and sovereign act.
source: Hexaemeron, Book I

excerpt: The earth was 'without form and void' — not as something evil but as raw material awaiting the shaping hand of God.
full_text: Basil explains that the earth in its initial state was not sinful or defective but simply unformed — like clay before the potter has worked it. The Spirit of God hovering over the waters signifies the divine care and preparation for what was to come. The formlessness was not a failure but the condition from which divine artistry would produce beauty and order.
source: Hexaemeron, Homily 2

excerpt: 'Let there be light' — God spoke and it was so, showing that His word is not mere sound but creative power.
full_text: Augustine meditates on 'Let there be light' in De Genesi ad Litteram: the light God created on the first day is not the light of the sun, which was made on the fourth day, but a more primordial light — perhaps spiritual or angelic illumination. The key theological point is that God creates by speaking: His word is not an empty sound but the very cause of being. What God wills, He speaks; what He speaks, comes to be.
source: De Genesi ad Litteram, Book I

excerpt: 'The Lord is my shepherd' — He who is God stoops to tend us as a shepherd tends his flock, showing the depth of divine condescension.
full_text: Augustine in his Exposition of the Psalms writes: David says 'The Lord rules me.' He does not say 'a lord' or 'my lord' but 'the Lord' absolutely — the one God, maker of heaven and earth, who has deigned to shepherd individual souls. The very fact that infinite majesty concerns itself with creatures so small as we are is itself cause for wonder and gratitude. To be shepherded by such a shepherd is to lack nothing.
source: Expositions on the Psalms, Psalm 23

excerpt: The good shepherd feeds his sheep on heavenly pastures — the body and blood of Christ at the altar are the green pastures of Psalm 23.
full_text: Ambrose interprets Psalm 23 with strong eucharistic resonance: the green pastures are the sacraments of the Church, the still waters are baptism, the table prepared is the altar. This is not merely a psalm of earthly comfort but a description of the spiritual nourishment available to the baptized Christian who partakes of Christ's body and blood. The shepherd who leads us to these waters is Christ himself, the great high priest and victim.
source: On the Sacraments, Book V

excerpt: The 'green pastures' are the divine scriptures and the doctrines of truth upon which the soul feeds and grows strong.
full_text: Origen in his homilies on the Psalms understands the green pastures spiritually: they are the lush meadows of holy scripture and orthodox teaching. The shepherd Christ leads us not to earthly food but to the nourishment of the soul — the words of eternal life. To lie down in green pastures is to rest in the truth of the gospel, fully satisfied by what God has revealed.
source: Homilies on the Psalms

excerpt: 'Though I walk through the valley of the shadow of death, I will fear no evil' — the presence of the shepherd is stronger than any darkness.
full_text: Chrysostom preaches that the valley of the shadow of death is present life itself — full of trials, dangers, and the constant nearness of mortality. Yet the psalmist does not say 'I will not enter the valley' but 'I will not fear evil.' The promise is not escape from difficulty but the divine companionship that makes difficulty bearable. The shepherd's rod and staff are the correction and comfort of God's word and providence.
source: Homilies on the Psalms

excerpt: 'Blessed are the poor in spirit' — not the materially destitute but those who are humble, who do not swell with pride.
full_text: Augustine in The Lord's Sermon on the Mount explains that 'poor in spirit' means the humble — those who do not think highly of themselves, who recognize their absolute dependence on God. This is the foundational beatitude: without humility, none of the other virtues can be properly cultivated. The kingdom of heaven is promised to the humble because pride was the original sin by which the devil fell, and humility is its remedy.
source: The Lord's Sermon on the Mount, Book I

excerpt: Christ begins the beatitudes with humility because it is the root of all the virtues — without it the rest cannot stand.
full_text: Chrysostom in his Homilies on Matthew marvels that Christ begins not with fasting, almsgiving, or prayer but with interior poverty of spirit. This is intentional: humility is the foundation. A man may fast and give alms while puffed up with pride, and all his works are vain. But he who is truly poor in spirit — who counts himself nothing before God — is already on the path to the kingdom.
source: Homilies on Matthew, Homily 15

excerpt: Those who mourn are blessed because they mourn the loss of the highest goods — truth and righteousness — which the world has forfeited.
full_text: Augustine explains that the mourning of the second beatitude is not mere grief over temporal losses but sorrow for sin and for the spiritual blindness of the world. The comforted mourners are those who grieve over their own sins and those of others, longing for the restoration of what was lost in the fall. Their consolation is the Holy Spirit — the Paraclete — who is promised to such souls.
source: The Lord's Sermon on the Mount, Book I

excerpt: The meek shall inherit the earth — not this transient earth but the new earth of the resurrection, the renewed creation promised to the saints.
full_text: Augustine notes the paradox: the world rewards the aggressive, the ambitious, the proud — yet Christ says the meek shall inherit the earth. This earth is not the present world in its fallen state but the transformed creation of the age to come, the inheritance of those who followed Christ in gentleness and patience. The meek do not grasp; they receive. They do not conquer; they are given.
source: The Lord's Sermon on the Mount, Book I

excerpt: To hunger and thirst after righteousness is to pursue virtue with the same intensity that a starving man pursues bread.
full_text: Chrysostom in his Homilies on Matthew says that Christ sets before us an image of great intensity: not a mild preference for goodness but a fierce, urgent, bodily craving for righteousness. Just as hunger and thirst allow no rest until satisfied, so the soul that truly loves virtue cannot be at peace while sin persists. These are the souls who shall be filled — completely, wholly satisfied — with the righteousness of God.
source: Homilies on Matthew, Homily 15

excerpt: Mercy is rewarded with mercy — what we show to others in need, God shows to us in our need before His judgment.
full_text: Augustine connects the beatitude of mercy directly to the Lord's Prayer: 'Forgive us as we forgive.' The merciful shall obtain mercy because they have already practiced toward others what they need from God. This is not a transaction but a formation: the soul that shows mercy has been enlarged, opened, made capable of receiving divine mercy. The hard and unmerciful heart cannot receive what it has refused to give.
source: The Lord's Sermon on the Mount, Book I

excerpt: 'Blessed are the pure in heart, for they shall see God' — the eye of the soul must be cleansed of earthly desires before it can bear the vision of God.
full_text: Augustine in De Trinitate and The Lord's Sermon on the Mount returns often to this beatitude: the vision of God is the supreme human happiness, but it requires purity of heart. The heart soiled by lust, greed, and pride cannot perceive God — not because God withholds himself, but because the soul's eye is not capable of such a vision. The whole Christian life is a purification — a gradual healing of the inner eye — so that in the end we may see God as He is.
source: The Lord's Sermon on the Mount, Book I; De Trinitate

excerpt: The Lord's Prayer is a summary of the whole gospel — in a few words Christ has compressed all that we need to pray and all that we need to be.
full_text: Tertullian in On Prayer calls the Lord's Prayer 'an epitome of the whole gospel.' In it Christ teaches us to address God as Father — proclaiming our adoption — and to seek His name's hallowing, His kingdom's coming, His will's accomplishment. Then He turns to our needs: daily bread, forgiveness, and deliverance from evil. No human prayer can improve on this template; it is the prayer of the Son given to His brothers and sisters.
source: On Prayer, Chapter 1

excerpt: By teaching us to say 'Our Father,' Christ makes us brothers and sisters to one another — the prayer is irreducibly communal.
full_text: Augustine in his Letter to Proba on prayer notes that the Lord's Prayer begins not 'My Father' but 'Our Father' — a small word that carries immense weight. When I pray this prayer I cannot pray it selfishly, for I am already praying for all who call God Father alongside me. The prayer forms us as a community before it forms us as individuals. To pray it honestly is to commit to the brotherhood and sisterhood of all the baptized.
source: Letter to Proba (Letter 130)

excerpt: We ask for our debts to be forgiven as we forgive our debtors — this clause is a condition, not merely a petition.
full_text: Cyprian in On the Lord's Prayer explains that 'forgive us as we forgive' is a solemn condition attached to the petition. We are asking God to measure His forgiveness by our own. If we are unforgiving toward our brothers, we are in effect asking God to be unforgiving toward us. This is why Christ follows the prayer with the warning: 'If you do not forgive men their trespasses, neither will your Father forgive yours.' The petition is also an examination of conscience.
source: On the Lord's Prayer, Chapter 23

excerpt: Peter is the rock insofar as he confessed Christ — and every soul who makes Peter's confession is likewise a rock on which the Church is built.
full_text: Origen in his Commentary on Matthew interprets 'upon this rock I will build my Church' as referring primarily to the confession of faith that Peter made. Any soul who confesses that Jesus is the Christ, the Son of the living God, becomes in a spiritual sense a 'Peter' — a rock — upon whom the Church is built. The authority to bind and loose is given to the Church as a whole insofar as she makes and lives by this confession.
source: Commentary on Matthew, Book XII

excerpt: The Church is one, and its unity flows from Peter — not that Peter is above the other apostles, but that unity must have a visible source.
full_text: Cyprian in On the Unity of the Church argues that Christ gave to Peter alone the keys and the promise about the rock specifically to illustrate the principle of unity. The other apostles had equal dignity and power, but the primacy was given to Peter so that the Church's oneness would be made manifest. Whoever abandons the chair of Peter abandons the Church herself, for a branch cut from the vine cannot bear fruit.
source: On the Unity of the Church, Chapter 4

excerpt: 'Baptizing them in the name of the Father and of the Son and of the Holy Spirit' — one name, three persons, the Trinity confessed in the very act of Christian initiation.
full_text: Augustine observes that Christ says 'in the name' (singular) of the Father and of the Son and of the Holy Spirit — not 'in the names.' The one name is the divine nature shared by the three persons. Baptism into this one name is the doorway into Trinitarian life: the Christian is plunged into the mystery of God who is Father, Son, and Spirit, and emerges from the waters as an adopted child, a brother of the incarnate Son, a temple of the Holy Spirit.
source: On the Trinity, Book XV

excerpt: 'In the beginning was the Word' — not in a temporal beginning, for the Word has no beginning of being; the Word simply was, coeternal with the Father.
full_text: Augustine writes in Homilies on John: 'In the beginning was the Word.' Which beginning? Not in a temporal beginning, as if the Word began to be at some time. For if the Word began, then it was not in the beginning; for what begins is not yet. But 'In the beginning was' — that is, already was before all things. The Word was not made, but all things were made by the Word.
source: Homilies on the Gospel of John, Tractate 1

excerpt: Why does John not say 'At first was the Word' but 'In the beginning'? To show that the Word has no beginning of existence.
full_text: Chrysostom in Homilies on John: Why does he not say 'At first'? Because 'in the beginning' points to an existence before all ages and time. Had he said 'from the first,' one might imagine a beginning. But 'was in the beginning' destroys all such imagination and shows the Son is without beginning.
source: Homilies on the Gospel of John, Homily 2

excerpt: The Word is the image of the invisible God — the Wisdom and Truth through whom all things were made.
full_text: Origen writes in his Commentary on John: We must understand 'Word' not as a mere sound but as the Wisdom, the Truth, the Life. The Word is with God, not as a lesser being beside a greater, but as one who shares the divine nature while remaining distinct in person. The preposition 'with' indicates both communion and distinction.
source: Commentary on the Gospel of John, Book 1

excerpt: 'He was in the beginning with God' — John repeats this to cut off every heretical path: the Word was never separated from God at any point.
full_text: Chrysostom comments: John having said 'In the beginning was the Word,' and 'the Word was with God,' and 'the Word was God,' now confirms all of this with 'He was in the beginning with God.' This repetition is not mere style but theological insistence: there is no moment, no state, no condition in which the Son was without the Father. He was always with God, always God.
source: Homilies on the Gospel of John, Homily 2

excerpt: 'All things were made through him' — not a single creature exists that was not made through the eternal Word.
full_text: Augustine in Tractates on John: 'All things were made through him.' John is emphatic: all things — without exception. The angels, the heavens, the earth, time, matter — all came into being through the Word. This excludes the Word himself from the category of 'things made,' for if all things were made through him, he himself was not made. He is the Creator, not a creature.
source: Homilies on the Gospel of John, Tractate 1

excerpt: 'In him was life' — life not received from another but life that is his own, the source from which all living things draw their being.
full_text: Augustine meditates: 'In him was life.' This life is not a gift given to the Word from outside but the Word's own inalienable property. All created things have life only by participation in this Life; the Word is Life itself. And this life is the light of men — for rational creatures are illuminated not merely by physical light but by the divine reason that shines in the Word.
source: Homilies on the Gospel of John, Tractate 1

excerpt: 'The darkness did not overcome it' — no power of sin or death or ignorance has been able to extinguish the light that shines in the Word.
full_text: Chrysostom writes: The light shines in the darkness and the darkness has never mastered it. All the forces of sin, death, and the devil could not put out the light of the Word. He became flesh and dwelt among us, and still the darkness could not overcome him. This is our confidence: the light we follow is inextinguishable.
source: Homilies on the Gospel of John, Homily 4

excerpt: The 'true light that enlightens every man' means the capacity to know God is a gift given to every rational soul.
full_text: Augustine explains: 'The true light enlightens every man coming into the world.' This light is not merely physical but rational and moral — it is the light of reason and conscience by which every human being has some capacity to know right from wrong and to seek God. The tragedy is that men love darkness more than light, for their deeds are evil. The light shines universally; the reception of the light depends on the will.
source: Homilies on the Gospel of John, Tractate 2

excerpt: 'Power to become children of God' — John says power, not necessity; adoption is offered to all but received only by faith.
full_text: Chrysostom comments on 'He gave them power to become children of God': The word 'power' is significant. John does not say that by receiving the Word we automatically become God's children, but that we are given the capacity, the authority, to become so. This gift is actualized by faith and by baptism. The adoption is real — not metaphorical — but it must be received and lived.
source: Homilies on the Gospel of John, Homily 10

excerpt: 'The Word was made flesh' — not by ceasing to be the Word, but by taking flesh into personal union with himself.
full_text: Cyril of Alexandria in his Commentary on John: The Word was not changed into flesh; the divine nature does not admit change. Rather, the Word assumed human nature into personal union — so that what is said of the flesh is said of the Word, and what is said of the Word is said of the flesh, because there is one person, one Christ.
source: Commentary on the Gospel of John, Book 1

excerpt: The Word became what we are so that we might become what He is — the incarnation is the foundation of our deification.
full_text: Athanasius in On the Incarnation states the purpose of the incarnation with luminous clarity: 'He became man so that we might become divine.' The Word took on human nature not to demean himself but to elevate us — to join our nature to his so that the corruption and death that had seized humanity might be destroyed from within, and divine life might flow back into what had become mortal.
source: On the Incarnation, Chapter 54

excerpt: 'Full of grace and truth' — grace because He gives what is undeserved; truth because He fulfills all the Old Testament types and shadows.
full_text: Augustine in Tractates on John: 'Full of grace and truth.' The Law was given by Moses; grace and truth came by Jesus Christ. The Law showed what we owe; grace gives what we cannot earn. The Law pointed to a truth not yet arrived; the incarnate Word is that truth in person. In Christ, grace and truth are not separated — He gives freely and He is himself the reality to which all symbols pointed.
source: Homilies on the Gospel of John, Tractate 2

excerpt: 'No one has seen God at any time' — the Father in His essence is invisible; the Son reveals Him because He shares the same essence.
full_text: Chrysostom: 'No man hath seen God at any time; the only begotten Son, which is in the bosom of the Father, He hath declared him.' The Father's essence is beyond all creaturely vision; even the highest angels cannot see God as He is in himself. But the Son, who is in the bosom of the Father — eternally in intimate union with Him — has made the Father known. To see the Son is to see the Father.
source: Homilies on the Gospel of John, Homily 15

excerpt: God loved the world not because it was beautiful but in order to make it beautiful — His love is the cause of our goodness, not its consequence.
full_text: Augustine in Tractates on John: 'For God so loved the world.' Observe that it does not say God loved the world as something beautiful. He did not love it because it was beautiful; He made it beautiful because He loved it. His love is not the consequence of our goodness but the cause of it. He gave his only-begotten Son that we who were dead might live through him.
source: Homilies on the Gospel of John, Tractate 12

excerpt: 'That whosoever believeth in him should not perish' — the offer is universal; the condition is faith, within the reach of all.
full_text: Chrysostom marvels at the scope of the promise: 'whosoever.' Not Jew or Greek, not noble or slave — but whosoever believes. The offer is genuinely universal. And the condition — belief — is not some heroic achievement but the simple act of the soul accepting what God has done. Perishing is the natural end of the fallen; eternal life is the gift given through faith in the Son.
source: Homilies on the Gospel of John, Homily 27

excerpt: 'I am the bread of life' — He who eats this bread will not hunger again, for this food satisfies the soul's deepest longing permanently.
full_text: Augustine in Tractates on John preaches: 'I am the bread of life.' He who comes to me shall not hunger; he who believes in me shall never thirst. The hunger that Christ satisfies is not of the belly but of the soul — the restless longing Augustine famously described: 'Our heart is restless until it rests in Thee.' Christ is the food that ends restlessness permanently, for in Him the soul finds what it was made for.
source: Homilies on the Gospel of John, Tractate 25

excerpt: 'The bread that I will give is my flesh' — the eucharist is truly the flesh of the incarnate Word, given for the life of the world.
full_text: Cyril of Alexandria insists against any merely symbolic interpretation: 'The bread which I will give is my flesh, which I will give for the life of the world.' The flesh of Christ in the eucharist is the same flesh that was incarnate of the Virgin, that was crucified, that rose from the dead. To receive this flesh is to receive the life-giving power of the Word united to our humanity. This is not a figure but a reality.
source: Commentary on the Gospel of John, Book 4

excerpt: 'I am the good shepherd' — the good shepherd is known by this: he lays down his life for his sheep of his own free will.
full_text: Augustine in Tractates on John: The hireling flees when the wolf comes because the sheep are not his and he cares nothing for them. But the good shepherd owns the sheep — purchased them with his own blood — and therefore he does not flee. He lays down his life. And lest we miss the point, Christ adds: 'No one takes it from me; I lay it down of my own accord.' The cross was not a defeat but a voluntary sacrifice.
source: Homilies on the Gospel of John, Tractate 46

excerpt: 'I and the Father are one' — one in essence, not merely one in will; this is the foundation of Nicene faith.
full_text: Athanasius in his Orations against the Arians returns repeatedly to this verse: 'I and the Father are one.' The Arians interpreted this as unity of will only — the Son agreeing with the Father's purposes. But Athanasius argues that 'one' here refers to unity of substance: the Son and the Father share one divine nature. This is confirmed by the Jewish reaction — they took up stones, because they understood him to claim divinity.
source: Orations against the Arians, Oration III

excerpt: 'I am the way, the truth, and the life' — there is no way to the Father except through the Son.
full_text: Cyprian appeals to this verse in On the Unity of the Church: He who does not hold the Church as his mother cannot have God as his father. For there is no way to the Father but through the Son, and the Son is present in His Church. Those who separate themselves from the body of Christ cut themselves off from the way, the truth, and the life — not because God is unwilling to save them, but because they have refused the means of salvation He has appointed.
source: On the Unity of the Church, Chapter 6

excerpt: Christ calls himself the way first because without the way, even those who know the truth and desire life cannot arrive at their destination.
full_text: Chrysostom notes the order: way, truth, life. Not truth and life first, then way — but way first. This is intentional: before we can possess the truth or enjoy the life, we must travel the road. And Christ himself is that road. He does not point us toward a path and stand aside; He is himself the path we walk. In walking with Him, we already possess truth and life, even on the journey.
source: Homilies on the Gospel of John, Homily 73

excerpt: 'The just shall live by faith' — faith is not the end of the journey but its beginning and its sustaining power all the way to the vision of God.
full_text: Augustine meditates on 'the just shall live by faith' throughout his writings: Faith is not a substitute for understanding but its starting point. We believe so that we may understand; we do not wait for understanding before we believe. The just person lives by faith — the whole Christian life, from conversion to death, is sustained by the act of entrusting oneself to God on the basis of His word rather than one's own sight.
source: On the Spirit and the Letter, Chapter 11

excerpt: 'Justified by faith, we have peace with God' — the peace Paul speaks of is the end of the enmity between God and the sinner that sin had created.
full_text: Chrysostom in Homilies on Romans: 'Therefore being justified by faith, we have peace with God.' The peace is not first a subjective sense of calm but an objective new relationship — the hostility that existed between the sinner and God has been abolished by the blood of Christ received through faith. From this peace flows the subjective experience of hope, joy, and the love of God poured out by the Spirit.
source: Homilies on Romans, Homily 9

excerpt: 'While we were yet sinners, Christ died for us' — the measure of God's love is that it loved the unlovable.
full_text: Augustine often returns to this verse: 'While we were yet sinners, Christ died for us.' Not when we had become righteous, not when we had earned it — while we were still enemies and rebels, He died. This is the scandal and the glory of divine love: it was not drawn out by our worthiness but poured out on our unworthiness. Here is the proof that God's love is not a response to our goodness but the source of it.
source: Homilies on the Gospel of John, Tractate 41

excerpt: 'No condemnation for those in Christ Jesus' — the sentence of death that hung over us has been cancelled by our union with Christ.
full_text: Chrysostom in Homilies on Romans exults: 'There is now therefore no condemnation to them which are in Christ Jesus.' The condemnation was real, the sentence was just, the judgment was deserved — and it has been fully borne by Christ. Those who are 'in Christ' — united to Him by faith and baptism — stand before God not under sentence but acquitted. Not merely pardoned, but declared righteous in the righteousness of the Son.
source: Homilies on Romans, Homily 13

excerpt: 'All things work together for good' — even trials and tribulations, God orchestrates for the benefit of those who love him.
full_text: Chrysostom comments: Paul does not say that all things are good, but that all things work together for good — which is a greater thing. Even what seems evil, even tribulations and trials, God orchestrates for the benefit of those who love him. The very fact that they love God is itself a gift of God; and having received this gift, they receive all things with it.
source: Homilies on Romans, Homily 15

excerpt: Even the sins of the predestined work together for their good — not because sin is good, but because God's mercy is greater than our failures.
full_text: Augustine writes in City of God: even the falls and failures of those whom God has predestined to glory are turned by God's providence to their ultimate good. This does not make sin harmless — it remains truly evil — but it means that God is never outmaneuvered. He writes straight with crooked lines, and the story of the elect is not undone by their failures.
source: City of God, Book XI

excerpt: Paul enumerates every possible power and declares that none of them can separate us from the love of God in Christ.
full_text: Chrysostom preaches in Homilies on Romans: See how Paul catalogs the universe — death and life, things present and things to come, height and depth, every conceivable creature — and sweeps them all aside. Not one of them, Paul says, has the power to separate the soul from God's love. This is not a naive optimism but the conclusion of a man who has suffered shipwreck, imprisonment, stoning, and exile. He speaks from experience.
source: Homilies on Romans, Homily 15

excerpt: 'Present your bodies as a living sacrifice' — not a one-time act but a life offered continuously to God.
full_text: Chrysostom in Homilies on Romans: The old covenant had dead sacrifices — animals slain on an altar. The new covenant asks for a living sacrifice — the Christian's entire bodily life, given over day by day to God. This means not killing the body through asceticism, but offering it: using its energies, its appetites, its strength, in the service of God and neighbor. The body is not the enemy of the soul; it is the instrument of worship.
source: Homilies on Romans, Homily 20

excerpt: Without charity, even speaking with the tongues of angels is mere noise — love is the form of all the virtues.
full_text: Augustine in On Christian Doctrine and many letters argues that caritas — ordered love — is the goal of all scripture and the soul of all Christian virtue. Paul's hymn to love in 1 Corinthians 13 shows that every gift, however spectacular, is empty without love. Augustine famously summarizes: 'Love God and do what you will' — meaning that the man who truly loves God will find that his will has been reformed by love into conformity with the divine will.
source: On Christian Doctrine, Book I

excerpt: 'God is love' — the most compact of all theological statements; if God is love, then to know God is to be transformed by love.
full_text: Augustine in the Homilies on the First Epistle of John: 'God is love.' These two words contain the whole of Christian theology. If you have grasped them you have grasped everything; if you have not, all your other theological knowledge profits you nothing. God is not merely loving — He is love itself, the very source and definition of love. To grow in love is to grow in God.
source: Homilies on the First Epistle of John, Homily 7

excerpt: 'I am the Alpha and the Omega' — Christ encompasses all of history: He is its beginning and its end, and all things find their meaning in Him.
full_text: Augustine in the City of God: The vision of John begins with the declaration 'I am the Alpha and the Omega.' History is not a random sequence of events but a story with an author who is also the beginning and the ending. Christ who was before creation and who will preside over its consummation gives meaning and direction to everything that happens between those two poles. Every page of human history is a page of His story.
source: City of God, Book XX

excerpt: 'A new heaven and a new earth' — not the annihilation of creation but its transformation; the world is renewed as gold is refined by fire.
full_text: Augustine in the City of God discusses the 'new heaven and new earth' of Revelation 21: This is not the abolition of the material world but its purification and transformation. Just as our bodies will be raised and glorified, so the creation itself — which groans in travail — will be renewed. The new Jerusalem is not a rejection of the earthly but its fulfilment: the City of God perfected, the dwelling of God with men, the final answer to all human longing.
source: City of God, Book XX
